body_txt: A subtrope of Don't Go in the Woods , this is when the woods are used for a particular form of Chase Scene . A villain is chasing their victim. The woods are handy for this type of thing for dramatic purposes: expect to see the victim hiding (usually for the purpose of a Hope Spot ) and appearing to escape, before the perpetrator sneaks up on them. The woods are useful because they provide ample coverage, but are generally extremely hard to navigate (and usually, though not always, a fair distance from a main road where you might come across a car). A lone tree root is also the most common cause of the most inconvenient Twisted Ankle , or a Bear Trap may be lurking in the undergrowth. It's also common for the woods to accommodate a lake or river so that the Chase Stops at Water . It will frequently be a man who chases a woman in terms of Slasher Movie logic (to the point where most subversions will play with the idea of a male pursuer). It may also be part of a Red Riding Hood Replica or a Little Dead Riding Hood riff, though it doesn't need to be. May at times coexist with a Pursued Protagonist or provide the setting for a Dramatic Chase Opening . This trope was given a horrific real-life riff by American serial killer Robert Hansen , who abducted women and pursued them through the wilderness. It's common for more procedural versions of this trope to borrow partly or wholly from Hansen's modus operandi: of kidnapping women and hunting them in the wilderness for Hunting the Most Dangerous Game reasons. As this trope is both too common and media-specific, no real-life examples, please .
